round1Verification.M1_open_redirect_strip_invalid_locale: fixed — Decision 5 + Task 3 + Testing Strategy spell out path normalization (collapse leading slashes, decode percent, reject backslash) and unit-test fixtures `/fr//evil.com` → `/evil.com`, backslash → reject
round1Verification.M2_302_referer_leak: fixed — Decision 5 + Task 3 verify-smoke explicitly require `X-Robots-Tag: noindex, nofollow` + `Referrer-Policy: no-referrer` set on the 302 response itself when after-strip path begins with `/auth/`
round1Verification.minor_prototype_pollution: fixed — Decision 9 names `Object.create(null)` plus filter for `__proto__`/`constructor`/`prototype`; test fixture in Testing Strategy line 200
round1Verification.minor_auth_middleware_fail_closed: fixed — Decision 6 explicitly states fail-closed on undefined baseName (treat as protected). Note: Round 1 also suggested passing through to 404 handler; rewriter chose fail-closed which is the more conservative security posture and acceptable

findings.title: Path-normalization step order in strip-invalid-locale not specified — decode-then-match vs match-then-decode produces different attack surfaces
findings.description: Decision 5 and Task 3 list three normalization operations (collapse multiple leading slashes, decode percent-encoding, reject backslash) but do not specify the order relative to the prefix-extraction regex `^/([a-z]{2})(?=/|$)`. If percent-decode runs BEFORE the regex match, a payload like `/%66r/dashboard` (where `%66` decodes to `f`) would not be caught by the lowercase-letter regex on raw input but could yield `/fr/dashboard` after decode — meaning the strip might run twice, or be skipped entirely depending on implementation. Conversely, if decode runs only on the after-strip suffix, `/fr/%2e%2e/admin` could decode to `/../admin` post-strip and produce an upward-traversal redirect target. The unit-test fixtures listed (lines 199-200) cover the happy-path collapse case but not the decode-ordering case.
findings.location: Decision 5 / Task 3 — strip-invalid-locale.ts; Testing Strategy line 199
findings.impact: Implementation ambiguity could ship a working-looking middleware with a residual bypass. Concrete risk is low (the redirect is same-origin only after collapse) but path-traversal in the redirect target could land an authenticated user on an unintended route, or skip the strip entirely.
findings.recommendation: Add to Decision 5 or Task 3 description an explicit order: (1) read `event.path` raw, (2) match prefix regex on raw path, (3) compute suffix = path.slice(3) [`/xx`.length], (4) decode percent on suffix only, (5) reject if decoded suffix contains `\` or `..` segment, (6) collapse multiple leading `/` to single `/`, (7) rebuild `Location` header. Add unit-test fixtures for `/fr/%2e%2e/admin` (decode-then-reject) and `/%66r/dashboard` (raw regex doesn't match → passthrough is OK because no `/fr` prefix on raw path).

findings.title: Auth-zone detection uses prefix `/auth/` (with trailing slash) — `/fr/auth` without trailing slash bypasses 302-response security headers
findings.description: Decision 5 specifies that auth-zone headers (`X-Robots-Tag`, `Referrer-Policy`) are set when `path-without-prefix` begins with `/auth/` (trailing slash). For a request to `/fr/auth` (no trailing slash, no path component beyond `auth`), the after-strip path is `/auth` which does NOT match `startsWith('/auth/')`. The 302 response would then be emitted without the security headers. Concrete attack surface is small because `/auth` alone is not a route that carries `?token=` parameters in the user-spec design (tokens live on `/auth/verify-email`, `/auth/reset-password`). However, an attacker could append `?token=x` to any URL: `/fr/auth?token=stolen` → 302 to `/auth?token=stolen` without `Referrer-Policy: no-referrer`, leaking via Referer if the unmatched route renders any external resource.
findings.impact: Edge-case bypass of token-leak protection on the 302 response itself. Low practical impact in current design but a defense-in-depth gap.
findings.recommendation: Change auth-zone match from `startsWith('/auth/')` to a regex `^/auth(/|$|\?)` or equivalent (`/auth` exactly, `/auth/...`, or `/auth?...`). Alternative simpler fix: apply the headers unconditionally on every 302 from this middleware — there is no downside (the middleware only fires on invalid-prefix redirects, none of which are crawler-friendly responses by design).

findings.title: routeRules security headers and middleware allow-list both hardcode `/en` — adding a third locale silently drops security parity
findings.description: Decision 5 hardcodes `allow-list = ['en']`. Decision 14 hardcodes `routeRules` key `/en/auth/**`. If a future epic adds a third locale (e.g. `de`), neither config will inherit security headers automatically — `/de/auth/verify-email?token=x` would pass through the middleware (allow-list match) but receive no `X-Robots-Tag`/`Referrer-Policy` because `routeRules` only widens to `/en/auth/**`. Tech-spec acknowledges 2-locale scope (user-spec constraint) so this is not a current vulnerability, but the design embeds a maintenance landmine.
findings.impact: Regression risk on future locale addition. No current exploit.
findings.recommendation: Either: (a) extract locale list to a shared constant `SUPPORTED_LOCALES` exported from a single module, with `nuxt.config.ts` deriving routeRules keys via a generator (`SUPPORTED_LOCALES.flatMap(l => [`/${l}/auth/**`, ...])`), and the middleware importing the same constant; OR (b) add an explicit comment in Decision 14 and the routeRules block: `// SECURITY: when adding a locale, also widen routeRules and middleware allow-list — ALL of /<locale>/auth/** must carry these headers`. Option (a) is mechanically safer.

findings.title: Production console-warn suppression for vue-i18n missing keys not stated as Acceptance Criterion
findings.description: Round 1 raised this as a minor (information disclosure of internal i18n key paths via `[intlify]` warnings in production browser console). The rewritten tech-spec mentions in Decision 10 that `te()` is used to avoid `t()` falling through to raw key strings, but does NOT explicitly require `silentTranslationWarn: true` and `silentFallbackWarn: true` in `i18n.config.ts` for production builds, nor adds an AC verifying zero `[intlify]` warnings on a production build. user-spec line 49 still mentions `console.warn у dev` — implementation could leave the default vue-i18n warnings active in prod.
findings.impact: Internal i18n key paths (e.g. `errors.login.401`) leak to production browser console — minor enumeration aid for an attacker mapping application surface.
findings.recommendation: Add to Task 2 description: `silentTranslationWarn: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production'`, `silentFallbackWarn: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production'` in `i18n.config.ts`. Add tech-spec AC: `production build's browser console contains zero [intlify] warnings on missing keys`. Verify via Playwright `page.on('console')` listener in the existing E2E spec or as a smoke check in Verify-smoke.

findings.title: Cookie `i18n_lang` is non-HttpOnly by design — trade-off is intentional but not documented in spec
findings.description: Decision 3 specifies `cookieKey: 'i18n_lang'`, `cookieMaxAge`, `cookieSecure` (env-conditional), `cookieSameSite: 'lax'`, but does not mention HttpOnly. The cookie is necessarily non-HttpOnly because client-side `setLocale()` writes it. Round 1 raised this — the rewritten spec did not add an explicit comment/AC clarifying the trade-off. Impact remains low (cookie carries no auth significance, only UX state) but future reviewers may flag this without context.
findings.impact: Documentation/maintainability gap; no actual security regression. The cookie's compromise via XSS has no consequence beyond locale-state disruption.
findings.recommendation: Add one sentence to Decision 3 rationale: `Cookie is intentionally non-HttpOnly because client-side setLocale() must read/write it; carries no auth significance — compromise has no security impact beyond UX disruption.` No code change needed.

owaspCoverage.A01_Broken_Access_Control: covered — Decision 6 (auth middleware fail-closed on undefined baseName, locale-aware route-name matching)
owaspCoverage.A02_Cryptographic_Failures: n/a — feature does not introduce new crypto
owaspCoverage.A03_Injection: covered — Decision 9 prototype-pollution defense in check-locales.mjs (Object.create(null), filter dangerous keys)
owaspCoverage.A04_Insecure_Design: covered — Decision 11 (Zod re-evaluation), Decision 12 (explicit navigateTo); minor finding on hardcoded locale list (maintenance landmine)
owaspCoverage.A05_Security_Misconfiguration: covered — Decision 14 (routeRules widen), Decision 5 (302-response headers); 3 minor findings on edge cases
owaspCoverage.A06_Vulnerable_Components: n/a at spec stage — `@nuxtjs/i18n@^9` and `@playwright/test@^1.49` are current; npm audit deferred to implementation
owaspCoverage.A07_Auth_Failures: covered — auth middleware refactor (Decision 6); existing token-based reset/verify flow is out of scope per Round 1 minor #8
owaspCoverage.A08_Software_Data_Integrity: covered — Decision 9 (build-time JSON validation, prebuild script as integrity gate); Round 1 minor on prebuild bypass remains relevant for future CI hardening but acceptable for current MVP
owaspCoverage.A09_Logging_Monitoring: minor finding — production vue-i18n warning suppression not in AC
owaspCoverage.A10_SSRF: n/a — no server-side requests with user-controlled URLs introduced
owaspCoverage.Hardcoded_Secrets: n/a — no credentials introduced; cookie name is a public identifier
